title: MDL Operator Discovery
subtitle: Compression as the Test of Primitivity — finding the right number of Schankian operators

children.children: This tutorial explores how Minimum Description Length (MDL) can discover the right set of semantic operators — not too few, not too many. The sweet spot of compression reveals true primitives.
How Many Primitives Do You Need?

Schank proposed ~14 conceptual primitives (ATRANS, PTRANS, PROPEL, MOVE, GRASP, INGEST, EXPEL, MTRANS, MBUILD, SPEAK, ATTEND, SMELL, FEEL, HARM). But why 14? Why not 5? Why not 500? This is the operator counting problem — and it turns out information theory gives us a principled answer.

props.analogy: Imagine you're writing a phrasebook for travelers. Too few phrases ('hello', 'help') and you can't communicate much — every conversation requires long explanations. Too many phrases (memorizing entire conversations) and the book is too heavy to carry. The perfect phrasebook has just enough common phrases to cover most situations compactly. MDL finds the perfect phrasebook for events.

children.children: With too few operators, you need long, complex descriptions to capture what happened. With too many operators, your 'language' of primitives is bloated with redundancy. The MDL sweet spot minimizes the TOTAL description: the cost of the operator dictionary PLUS the cost of encoding events using those operators.

props.title: Why can't we just cluster verbs and count the clusters?

children.children: Clustering gives you groups, but how many clusters? K-means needs K as input — that's the question we're trying to answer! The elbow method and silhouette scores are heuristics. MDL gives a principled, information-theoretic answer: the number of operators that minimally describes the data.
More importantly, MDL finds compositional structure. 'Chop' might decompose into CONTACT + SEPARATE — clustering would just put 'chop' in one cluster, missing the internal structure entirely.

props.title: 2. Minimum Description Length — The Core Idea

children.children: The Best Theory Compresses the Data Most

MDL (Rissanen, 1978) says: the best model is the one that most compresses the data. Total description length = model complexity + data encoded using the model. This is a formalization of Occam's Razor.

definitions.definition: How many bits to describe the operator dictionary itself. More operators = higher model cost. Each operator needs its own description: what it does, its type signature, its selectional restrictions.
How many bits to encode all events using the operators. Fewer operators = higher data cost because each event needs longer descriptions (compositions, exceptions, special cases).
L(M) + L(D|M) — the quantity we minimize. The sweet spot balances model complexity against encoding efficiency.
The theoretical minimum description length of a string — the shortest program that produces it. MDL approximates this uncomputable ideal with practical two-part codes.

props.title: MDL in Action: Describing Kitchen Events

children.children: Suppose we have 100 kitchen events: chopping, dicing, slicing, mincing, grating, peeling, coring, trimming...
**1 operator (MODIFY):** Model cost = 1 bit. But each event needs many extra bits to specify HOW: 'MODIFY + sharp-tool + downward + repeated + into-pieces' for chopping. Data cost is huge.
**100 operators (one per verb):** Model cost = ~7 bits × 100 = 700 bits. Each event is just one operator name (~7 bits). Data cost is minimal, but model cost dominates.

**~5 operators (SEPARATE, REMOVE, REDUCE, COMBINE, TRANSFORM):** Model cost = ~35 bits. Most events need 1-2 operators: 'chop' = SEPARATE, 'peel' = REMOVE. Total description is minimized. These ARE the primitives.

props.title: 🎛️ The MDL Sweet Spot
props.renderCode: const n = state.numOperators; const modelCost = n * 8; const dataCost = Math.round(500 / Math.sqrt(n) + 20 * Math.max(0, 1 - n/5)); const total = modelCost + dataCost; const minTotal = Math.min(...Array.from({length: 50}, (_, i) => (i+1)*8 + Math.round(500/Math.sqrt(i+1) + 20*Math.max(0, 1-(i+1)/5)))); const optK = Array.from({length: 50}, (_, i) => i+1).reduce((best, k) => { const t = k*8 + Math.round(500/Math.sqrt(k) + 20*Math.max(0, 1-k/5)); return t < best.t ? {k, t} : best; }, {k:1, t:Infinity}); const barW = 280; const maxVal = 900; const mW = Math.round(modelCost/maxVal*barW); const dW = Math.round(dataCost/maxVal*barW); const isOptimal = Math.abs(total - minTotal) < 5; return React.createElement('div', {style:{fontFamily:'monospace',fontSize:14}}, React.createElement('div', {style:{marginBottom:12}}, React.createElement('span', {style:{fontWeight:'bold'}}, `K = ${n} operators`), isOptimal ? React.createElement('span', {style:{color:'#22c55e',marginLeft:8}}, '✨ OPTIMAL!') : null), React.createElement('div', {style:{marginBottom:6}}, 'Model cost L(M): ', React.createElement('span',{style:{color:'#f59e0b',fontWeight:'bold'}}, modelCost + ' bits')), React.createElement('div', {style:{display:'flex',height:24,width:barW,background:'#1e293b',borderRadius:4,overflow:'hidden',marginBottom:8}}, React.createElement('div', {style:{width:mW,background:'#f59e0b'}})), React.createElement('div', {style:{marginBottom:6}}, 'Data cost L(D|M): ', React.createElement('span',{style:{color:'#3b82f6',fontWeight:'bold'}}, dataCost + ' bits')), React.createElement('div', {style:{display:'flex',height:24,width:barW,background:'#1e293b',borderRadius:4,overflow:'hidden',marginBottom:8}}, React.createElement('div', {style:{width:dW,background:'#3b82f6'}})), React.createElement('div', {style:{marginBottom:6,fontSize:16,fontWeight:'bold'}}, 'TOTAL: ', React.createElement('span',{style:{color: isOptimal ? '#22c55e' : '#ef4444'}}, total + ' bits')), React.createElement('div', {style:{display:'flex',height:24,width:barW,background:'#1e293b',borderRadius:4,overflow:'hidden',marginBottom:12}}, React.createElement('div', {style:{width:mW,background:'#f59e0b'}}), React.createElement('div', {style:{width:dW,background:'#3b82f6'}})), React.createElement('div', {style:{fontSize:12,color:'#94a3b8'}}, `Optimal: K=${optK.k} (${optK.t} bits). ${n < optK.k ? 'Too few operators — data cost dominates' : n > optK.k ? 'Too many operators — model cost dominates' : 'Perfect balance!'}`));

children.children: Operators as a Grammar for Events

MDL isn't just about counting operators — it discovers their compositional structure. This connects to grammar induction: finding the rules that generate observed sequences. Event sequences have grammar too: 'John bought coffee' follows the pattern ATRANS(buyer, seller, goods, money).

props.analogy: Think of ZIP file compression. ZIP doesn't just find repeated strings — it builds a dictionary of patterns and replaces them with short codes. The dictionary IS the grammar. MDL does the same for events: the 'dictionary entries' that best compress event descriptions ARE the semantic primitives. A ZIP file with a good dictionary is small; a theory with good operators is compact.

children.children: The SEQUITUR algorithm (Nevill-Manning & Witten, 1997) does exactly this for symbol sequences: it discovers hierarchical grammar rules that compress the input. Apply it to event sequences and you get operator composition rules for free.
SEQUITUR scans a sequence and whenever a pair of symbols appears twice, it creates a rule. Applied to event descriptions:
Input: GRASP+MOVE+RELEASE, GRASP+MOVE+RELEASE, GRASP+MOVE+HOLD

SEQUITUR discovers: Rule A → GRASP+MOVE (appears 3×), then the sequence becomes: A+RELEASE, A+RELEASE, A+HOLD. Rule A IS a composite operator — what we might call TRANSPORT. The algorithm discovered it purely from compression.

Consider 1000 restaurant visit descriptions. Without operators, each needs ~200 words. With the 'restaurant script' operator RESTAURANT_VISIT = ENTER → SIT → ORDER → EAT → PAY → LEAVE, most descriptions compress to: RESTAURANT_VISIT(person=John, restaurant=Café, food=pasta, payment=card) — about 30 words worth of information.
The script saved ~170 words × 1000 events = 170,000 words. The script itself costs ~50 words to define. Net compression: 169,950 words. MDL says: this script is worth having.

But a 'Tuesday restaurant visit where it rains' script? Maybe saves 5 extra words per relevant event (50 events), but costs 60 words to define. Net: -310 words. MDL says: NOT worth having. Too specific.
True Primitives Are Incompressible
Kolmogorov complexity K(x) is the length of the shortest program that produces x. It's the theoretical minimum description length — but it's uncomputable! MDL is the practical approximation.

Here's the key insight for operators: a TRUE primitive has high Kolmogorov complexity relative to its length — you can't compress it further into simpler operators. ATRANS (abstract transfer of possession) is primitive because there's no simpler decomposition. But 'buy' = ATRANS(goods, seller→buyer) + ATRANS(money, buyer→seller) — it's compressible into two ATRANSes.

Kolmogorov complexity is uncomputable — you can never prove you've found the shortest program. MDL sidesteps this by fixing a model class (e.g., operator grammars) and finding the best model within that class. It's like saying: 'Among all possible operator sets of this form, which one compresses events best?'

props.title: 🧬 Primitive vs. Composite — The Compression Test

Irreducible.'},{name:'devour',decomp:'ATTEND + GRASP + INGEST (rapid, complete)',complexity:'LOW — composite',color:'#ef4444',desc:'INGEST with manner modifications.'}]; const v = verbs[state.selectedVerb]; return React.createElement('div', {style:{fontFamily:'monospace',fontSize:14}}, React.createElement('div', {style:{fontSize:20,fontWeight:'bold',color:v.color,marginBottom:12}}, v.name), React.createElement('div', {style:{marginBottom:8}}, React.createElement('span',{style:{color:'#94a3b8'}}, 'Decomposition: '), v.decomp ? React.createElement('span',{style:{color:'#fbbf24'}}, v.decomp) : React.createElement('span',{style:{color:'#22c55e',fontStyle:'italic'}}, '∅ — irreducible')), React.createElement('div', {style:{marginBottom:8}}, React.createElement('span',{style:{color:'#94a3b8'}}, 'Kolmogorov complexity: '), React.createElement('span',{style:{color:v.color,fontWeight:'bold'}}, v.complexity)), React.createElement('div', {style:{padding:12,background:'#1e293b',borderRadius:8,marginTop:12,borderLeft:`3px solid ${v.color}`}}, v.desc), React.createElement('div', {style:{marginTop:16,fontSize:12,color:'#64748b'}}, 'A true primitive resists decomposition — its shortest description IS itself.'));

children.children: MDL Unifies Multiple Operator Discovery Methods
MDL isn't isolated — it connects to several other frameworks for finding the right operators:

definitions.definition: The IB curve IS the rate-distortion curve. Both ask: 'How much can you compress while preserving what matters?' IB does it with mutual information; MDL does it with description length. Same answer, different math.
DreamCoder's sleep phase refactors programs to extract reusable primitives — this IS MDL compression of a program library. The library cost = L(M), the program lengths = L(D|M).

RG coarse-graining progressively removes detail until only fixed-point operators remain. MDL formalizes when to STOP coarse-graining: when further abstraction costs more model complexity than it saves in data encoding.
MDL and Bayesian model comparison are asymptotically equivalent. The MDL optimal model = the model with highest marginal likelihood. Operator count selection = Bayesian model comparison.

props.title: MDL vs. BIC vs. AIC — which to use?

children.children: AIC (Akaike) penalizes by 2k (number of parameters). BIC (Bayesian) penalizes by k·log(n). MDL uses the actual codelength. For operator discovery:

AIC tends to overcount operators (too lenient). BIC is closer to MDL but assumes the true model is in the model class. MDL makes no such assumption — it's a pure compression criterion. For discovering semantic primitives, MDL is most principled because we DON'T know the true model.

props.title: 6. A Concrete Algorithm for Operator Discovery

children.children: The MDL Operator Discovery Pipeline
Here's how you'd actually use MDL to discover Schankian operators from a corpus of event descriptions:

Step 1: Encode events as (subject, verb-phrase, object, result-state) tuples. Step 2: Start with K=1 operator (everything is DO). Step 3: Split the operator that reduces total MDL most. Step 4: Repeat until no split reduces MDL. Step 5: Try merging similar operators. Step 6: The surviving set = your discovered primitives.

props.title: 🔬 MDL Operator Discovery Simulation
props.renderCode: const steps = [{k:1,ops:['DO'],mdl:950,split:'DO → PHYSICAL_DO + MENTAL_DO',desc:'Everything is one operator. Data cost is enormous — every event needs pages of detail.'},{k:2,ops:['PHYSICAL_DO','MENTAL_DO'],mdl:620,split:'PHYSICAL_DO → MOVE + CHANGE',desc:'Physical vs mental split. Huge MDL drop! This distinction compresses well.'},{k:4,ops:['MOVE','CHANGE','THINK','COMMUNICATE'],mdl:410,split:'MOVE → TRANSFER + TRANSPORT',desc:'Four operators. Each split still saves more in data cost than it adds in model cost.'},{k:7,ops:['TRANSFER','TRANSPORT','MODIFY','CREATE','THINK','PERCEIVE','COMMUNICATE'],mdl:310,split:'TRANSFER → ATRANS + PTRANS',desc:'Seven operators. Splits are getting smaller but still net positive.'},{k:12,ops:['ATRANS','PTRANS','PROPEL','MOVE','GRASP','INGEST','EXPEL','MTRANS','MBUILD','SPEAK','ATTEND','HARM'],mdl:265,split:'(checking merges...)',desc:'Twelve operators. Approaching the minimum. Further splits barely help.'},{k:14,ops:['ATRANS','PTRANS','PROPEL','MOVE','GRASP','INGEST','EXPEL','MTRANS','MBUILD','SPEAK','ATTEND','SMELL','FEEL','HARM'],mdl:260,split:'No beneficial splits or merges remain.',desc:'MDL minimum reached at ~14. Further splits INCREASE total description length. These are your primitives!'}]; const s = steps[state.step]; const barMax = 950; const barW = 260; const w = Math.round(s.mdl/barMax*barW); return React.createElement('div', {style:{fontFamily:'monospace',fontSize:13}}, React.createElement('div', {style:{fontSize:16,fontWeight:'bold',marginBottom:8}}, `Step ${state.step}: K = ${s.k} operators`), React.createElement('div', {style:{display:'flex',flexWrap:'wrap',gap:4,marginBottom:12}}, s.ops.map((op,i) => React.createElement('span', {key:i,style:{background:'#1e40af',color:'white',padding:'2px 8px',borderRadius:4,fontSize:11}}, op))), React.createElement('div', {style:{marginBottom:4}}, 'Total MDL: ', React.createElement('span', {style:{fontWeight:'bold',color: s.mdl <= 265 ? '#22c55e' : '#fbbf24'}}, s.mdl + ' bits')), React.createElement('div', {style:{height:20,width:barW,background:'#1e293b',borderRadius:4,overflow:'hidden',marginBottom:12}}, React.createElement('div', {style:{height:20,width:w,background: s.mdl <= 265 ? '#22c55e' : '#3b82f6',transition:'width 0.3s'}})), React.createElement('div', {style:{fontSize:12,color:'#94a3b8',marginBottom:8}}, 'Next: ', s.split), React.createElement('div', {style:{padding:10,background:'#0f172a',borderRadius:8,borderLeft:'3px solid #6366f1',fontSize:12}}, s.desc));

children.children: MDL Answers the Deepest Question

The deepest criticism of Schank's primitives was always: 'Why THESE primitives?' MDL provides the answer: because they maximally compress event descriptions. Not because a linguist intuited them, not because they're aesthetically pleasing, but because they're the optimal compression dictionary for human experience.

props.analogy: Why does the periodic table have ~118 elements? Because that's how many stable configurations of protons/neutrons exist. Similarly, why ~14 operators? Because that's how many distinct state-transformation types you need to compactly describe what happens in the world. It's not arbitrary — it's the compression optimum of reality.

children.children: Key insight: If independently trained MDL systems on different languages converge to the same ~14 operators, that's evidence for universal semantic primitives — the linguistic equivalent of discovering the periodic table.

questions.question: What does MDL minimize?
options: The number of operators only
The data encoding cost only
Model complexity + data encoded using the model
The maximum likelihood of the data
questions.explanation: MDL minimizes L(M) + L(D|M) — the total description length. Too few operators means high data cost; too many means high model cost. The minimum is the sweet spot.

questions.question: Why is Kolmogorov complexity uncomputable?
options: It requires infinite memory
No algorithm can find the shortest program for every string
It only works for binary strings
It requires quantum computers
questions.explanation: The halting problem prevents us from systematically finding the shortest program that produces a given output. MDL sidesteps this by fixing a model class and optimizing within it.

questions.question: How does MDL relate to the Information Bottleneck?
options: They're completely unrelated approaches
MDL is a special case of IB
The IB rate-distortion curve IS the MDL tradeoff from a different mathematical perspective
IB always finds more operators than MDL
questions.explanation: Both ask 'how much can you compress while preserving what matters?' IB uses mutual information; MDL uses description length. They converge to the same answer — the optimal operator set.

questions.question: What makes a true semantic primitive according to MDL?
options: It appears most frequently in text
A linguist labels it as primitive
It has high Kolmogorov complexity relative to its length — it can't be decomposed further
It maps to a single neuron in the brain
questions.explanation: A primitive resists further compression. 'Buy' decomposes into two ATRANSes, but ATRANS itself has no simpler decomposition. Its shortest description IS itself.

questions.question: If MDL finds ~14 operators independently across English, Mandarin, and Swahili corpora, what does this suggest?
options: The corpora were too small
The algorithm has a bug that always outputs 14
There are universal semantic primitives — the compression optimum of human experience
These languages borrowed from each other
questions.explanation: Cross-linguistic convergence to the same compression optimum would be strong evidence for universal primitives — like discovering the same chemical elements on different planets.
